preFix.title: Using Freecycle, Olio, and Buy Nothing groups effectively
preFix.subtitle: How community sharing networks work and what makes offers and requests succeed
preFix.excerpt: Freecycle, Olio, and Buy Nothing groups collectively divert thousands of tonnes of usable items from landfill each year in the UK. A well-written offer with a clear photo gets collected within hours; a vague one with no image sits for days or is ignored. This explains how each platform works, what items do and do not move well, and how to write an offer or request that results in a successful handover.
preFix.sourceNotes: Freecycle Network UK guidance (public). Olio platform documentation (public). Buy Nothing Project official guidance on group etiquette and participation. WRAP material diversion case studies.

content.text: Three distinct platforms serve community sharing in the UK, each with different scope and mechanics:

Freecycle (freecycle.org): an email-list-based network of local groups. Items are offered or requested via email to a group moderated by a volunteer. Collection is arranged by private reply. Over 700 groups across the UK. Suitable for furniture, appliances, garden materials, books, clothing.

Olio (olioapp.com): a smartphone-app platform covering both physical items and surplus food. Items listed with photos; neighbours within a set radius are notified. Includes a volunteer Food Waste Hero scheme in which volunteers collect food from supermarkets and list it on the app for local collection.

Buy Nothing Project (Facebook groups, also standalone app): hyperlocal groups based on specific postcodes or neighbourhoods. Operates as a gift economy: no swapping, no monetary exchange. Members offer, ask, and express gratitude. Items tend toward everyday household goods and children's items.
What moves well on these platforms
Children's items: clothing, toys, equipment, books. Outgrown quickly and typically in good condition. High demand in active Buy Nothing groups.
Furniture: tables, shelving, chairs, flat-pack that has already been built. Moves fast if the dimensions and condition are clear.
Garden materials: pots, compost, surplus seeds, paving slabs, topsoil. Freecycle is particularly active for these.
Surplus food: jars of home-made jam, garden surplus, dried goods approaching best-before. Olio is the most appropriate platform.
Include a photo. Listings with a clear, well-lit photo are collected 3-5 times faster than text-only listings. Take the photo in daylight against a plain background if possible.
State the exact condition. 'Good' or 'used' does not convey enough. 'Scratched on right side, otherwise fully functional' is specific and prevents failed collections.
Give dimensions for furniture and large items. Collections fall through when the item does not fit in the vehicle; dimensions prevent this.
State a collection window. 'Available this weekend, Saturday or Sunday' gives collectors time to arrange transport without the item sitting listed for weeks.

A request is more likely to succeed if it is specific and explains why the item is needed. 'Looking for a baby gate, 80-90 cm wide, for top of stairs, expecting our second child' gets more responses than 'wanted: baby gate'. Active community members respond more readily when requests feel personal rather than transactional.

attrs.fix: Message the person once to confirm, then offer to the next person if no reply within a reasonable window. On Freecycle, re-post after one failed collection attempt. On Olio, mark the collection slot as missed and offer to the next request.
attrs.cause: This is a known problem on all sharing platforms; no-shows are common
attrs.symptom: Collecting person does not show up and does not message

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
